## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

552	// ============================================================================
553	// TIER 1: WHY AND WHAT
554	// ============================================================================
555	
556	== System Overview
557	
558	=== Core Trust Challenge
559	
560	Modern development increasingly requires running foreign code with access to local work.
561	{st_container}-based development tools processing source code, local AI/ML tools analyzing proprietary data, and third-party services needing controlled network access create fundamental security challenges.
562	
563	This creates a fundamental trust dilemma:
564	
565	*
566	{st_container_s}
567	provide powerful tool isolation
568	* But they need controlled access to do useful work
569	* Standard
570	{st_container}
571	networking grants unfettered outbound access or else network services completely unavailable.
572	
573	=== The Bottle Pattern
574	
575	{at_rbm_system}
576	solves this by creating two simple, robust boundaries around a certain
577	{st_container}
578	type here referred to as a
579	{at_bottle_container}:
580	
581	. File Access Boundary
582	*
583	{at_bottle_container}
584	sees only explicitly controlled set of
585	{st_volume_mount_s}
586	* No other access to
587	{at_workstation}
588	filesystem
589	
590	. Network Boundary
591	*
592	{at_bottle_container}
593	has no direct network access
594	* All traffic flows through dedicated
595	{at_sentry_container},
596	a
597	{st_container}
598	configured with very tight network access and visibility controls.
599	* Only declared
600	{st_port_map_s}
601	and destinations allowed
602	* Full logging of allowed and denied traffic
603	
604	Each
605	{rbsc_crucible}
606	maintains these boundaries through a three-container architecture:
607	
608	* One
609	{at_sentry_container}
610	enforcing network policy on dual networks (bridge and enclave)
611	* One
612	{at_pentacle_container}
613	establishing and configuring the network namespace
614	* One
615	{at_bottle_container}
616	running the actual service, sharing the pentacle's network namespace via compose `network_mode: "service:pentacle"`
617	* Clear configuration in a single
618	{at_nameplate}
619	file, extended by per-nameplate compose fragments for volume mounts
620	
621	Docker Compose orchestrates the container lifecycle declaratively.
622	A base compose file (`rbob_compose.yml`) defines the three-container topology with health check chains: the
623	{at_sentry_container}
624	must be healthy before the
625	{at_pentacle_container}
626	starts, and the
627	{at_pentacle_container}
628	must be healthy before the
629	{at_bottle_container}
630	starts.
631	This prevents firewall bypass — the bottle cannot send traffic before security policies are enforced.
632	The "first packet" guarantee is enforceable because sentry's iptables are installed before the pentacle is healthy, the pentacle's namespace is shared with the bottle (so the bottle inherits the routing-and-firewall state already configured), and sentry sits on every inbound path by virtue of the published-port directive being on the sentry service (not pentacle) and the bottle's default route being via sentry.
633	Topology changes that violate any of these commitments require re-validation against the
634	{at_recipe_bottle}
635	Ifrit suite before declaring the security properties preserved.
636	Per-nameplate compose fragments (e.g., `«moniker».compose.yml`) add volume mount declarations.
637	Env-files passed via `--env-file` serve a dual purpose: YAML interpolation (`${VAR}` references in compose files resolve image refs, subnet, container names) and container environment forwarding (bare variable names in `environment:` sections inject values into containers at runtime).
638	
639	The
640	{at_pentacle_container}
641	is a privileged container that configures routing to ensure all traffic flows through the
642	{at_sentry_container},
643	then shares this pre-configured namespace with the
644	{at_bottle_container}
645	via compose `network_mode: "service:pentacle"`.
646	This ensures security policies are enforced from the first packet, and the
647	{at_bottle_container}
648	experiences only a functional path to its
649	{at_sentry_container}
650	gateway
651	
652	=== Security Properties
653	
654	The
655	{at_rbm_system}
656	maintains following essential guarantees:
657	
658	. File Access Control
659	*
660	{at_bottle_container_s}
661	can access only explicitly configured
662	{st_volume_mount_s}
663	* No access to broader system resources
664	
665	. Network Isolation
666	* All traffic flows through dedicated
667	{at_sentry_container}
668	* Each permitted traffic flow maintains exactly one controlled pathway
669	* Precise ingress, forwarding, and NAT rules enforce strict boundaries
670	* Only explicitly configured
671	{st_port_map_s}
672	allow traffic between
673	{at_transit_network}
674	and
675	{at_enclave_network}
676	*
677	{at_bottle_container_s}
678	have no direct network access; all connectivity flows through
679	{at_sentry_container_s}
680	* Comprehensive traffic logging
681	
682	. Service Separation
683	* Each
684	{rbsc_crucible}
685	fully isolated
686	* No cross-service communication
687	* Independent security policies
688	* Clean state separation
689	
690	. Service Access Control
691	* External access to services only permitted when
692	{rbrn_entry_mode}
693	is enabled
694	* No inter-container communication allowed within pods
695	* All service access must originate from outside the pod
696	* Entry traffic flows: external -> pod ->
697	{at_sentry_container}
698	->
699	{at_bottle_container}
700	
701	These properties hold across all operations and failure modes.
702	They depend on a layered-defense structure in which each layer is independently load-bearing for at least one invariant: network namespace isolation, capability bounds on the
703	{at_bottle_container}
704	(`cap_drop: NET_RAW, NET_ADMIN`), kernel reverse-path filtering, iptables filter chains (RBM-INGRESS, RBM-EGRESS, RBM-FORWARD), iptables NAT (PREROUTING DNAT, POSTROUTING MASQUERADE), and conntrack state tracking.
705	Removing or weakening a layer requires Ifrit-suite empirical validation to confirm the security envelope remains intact; architectural reasoning alone has been empirically insufficient.
706	
707	=== Adversarial Validation: The Ifrit
708	
709	include::RBSIP-ifrit_pentester.adoc[]
710	
711	== Getting Started
712	
713	For builders and operators new to
714	{at_recipe_bottle},
715	this section provides conceptual grounding and the setup journey.
716	
717	include::RBSGS-GettingStarted.adoc[leveloffset=+2]
718	
719	// ============================================================================
720	// TIER 2: HOW
721	// ============================================================================
722	
723	== Google API Engagement
724	
725	{at_recipe_bottle}
726	invokes
727	{gcp_service}
728	APIs through
729	{giam_service_account}
730	JWT authentication and direct HTTPS REST calls.
731	
732	* *Authentication*:
733	{giam_service_account_s}
734	use private key credentials to sign JWTs with RS256.
735	These JWTs are exchanged for
736	{oauth_token_s}
737	via Google's token endpoint.
738	All API calls include the bearer token in the Authorization header.
739	
740	* *Invocation Pattern*:
741	Operations execute through curl with JSON payloads and responses.
742	Each call captures both HTTP status codes and response bodies to files for processing.
743	Success is determined by HTTP 200/201/204 codes.
744	
745	* *Long-Running Operations*:
746	{gcp_service}
747	{gcp_lro_s}
748	return operation IDs that must be polled until completion.
749	The system polls at configurable intervals until completion.
750	The system handles both success and error states.
751	Operations utilize
752	{rbbc_submit}
753	and
754	{rbbc_await}
755	to handle all asynchronous operations.
756	
757	* *IAM Policies*:
758	All IAM policy modifications enforce version 3 to ensure conditional role binding support across all
759	{gcp_service}
760	resources.
761	
762	* *IAM Member Format*:
763	All IAM member bindings use `serviceAccount:«EMAIL»` format.
764	+
765	GCP IAM policy `members` fields only accept email-format identifiers (`serviceAccount:name@project.iam.gserviceaccount.com`).
766	The numeric `uniqueId` returned by `serviceAccounts.create` is valid for *API URL paths* (e.g., `projects/-/serviceAccounts/«UNIQUE_ID»` for GET/verification) but *not* for policy binding members.
767	`serviceAccount:«NUMERIC_ID»` returns HTTP 400 "Invalid service account" on all setIamPolicy endpoints — project, repository, and bucket alike.
768	+
769	Google's documentation recommends numeric IDs "to identify a service account just after it is created" (link:https://docs.google.com/iam/docs/manage-access-service-accounts[Manage access to service accounts]), but this applies to API resource addressing, not to IAM policy member strings.
770	The internal IAM system resolves email→numeric transparently; callers must use email.
771	+
772	Google-provisioned service agents (Cloud Build) are not affected — they are managed by Google, not newly created.
773	
774	== Operational Invariants
775	
776	=== Concurrent Session Safety
777	
778	{at_recipe_bottle}
779	commands are designed for concurrent execution from multiple independent sessions against the same filesystem.
780	This enables parallel workflows where different developers or automation systems invoke commands simultaneously.
781	
782	Concurrency safety is achieved through:
783	
784	* *Ephemeral Isolation*:
785	Each command invocation creates a unique temporary directory using `BURD_NOW_STAMP` (timestamp + process ID + random component), ensuring intermediate files never collide.
786	
787	* *Unique Artifact Naming*:
788	Cloud resources (build IDs, inscribe timestamps) incorporate unique identifiers, preventing overwrite conflicts in
789	{rbtgi_repository}.
790	
791	* *Read-Only Git Queries*:
792	Git operations are non-mutating (status checks, metadata reads).
793	The `git fetch` operation uses git's internal locking for safe concurrent execution.
794	
795	* *Semantic Preconditions*:
796	Operations like
797	{rbtgo_ark_conjure}
798	require clean git state.
799	This is a workflow constraint ensuring reproducible builds, not a collision guard.
800	
801	NOTE: Concurrent builds of the same vessel produce independent images with different timestamps.
802	Both builds succeed; neither overwrites the other.
803	
804	NOTE: The
805	{gcb_service}
806	private pool enforces a *Concurrent Build CPUs (Private Pool)* quota per project per region.
807	Machine type is set at pool creation time via
808	{rbrd_gcb_machine_type}
809	(a Compute Engine type such as `e2-standard-2`); individual build requests do not specify machine type.
810	If the pool's vCPU allocation is nearly exhausted, a second
811	build invocation will remain in `QUEUED` status while the first is `WORKING`.
812	The
813	{rbrr_gcb_min_concurrent_builds}
814	regime variable declares the operator's minimum concurrency requirement.
815	See
816	{rbtgo_quota_build}
817	for the manual Console procedure to review build capacity settings.
818	
819	== Key Premises
820	
821	[[rbsk_no_gcloud]]
822	//axl_voices axk_premise
823	{rbsk_no_gcloud}::
824	{at_recipe_bottle}
825	scripts MUST NOT invoke the `gcloud` CLI in local bash scripts or transitively through helper scripts.
826	All local
827	{gcp_service}
828	interaction uses direct REST API calls via `curl` with JWT-authenticated bearer tokens.
829	+
830	**Exception — vouch provenance fetch**: The {rbtgo_ark_vouch} verification step (conjure mode only) invokes `gcloud artifacts docker images describe --show-provenance` for SLSA provenance envelope retrieval.
831	This wraps complex Container Analysis API interaction that would be burdensome to replicate via raw REST.
832	All other GCB step operations use the GCE metadata server for OAuth token acquisition and direct registry REST API calls — no gcloud.
833	The provenance fetch runs as Python 3 on the gcloud builder image after the primary image is built; the reproducibility concern that motivates this premise is weaker for post-build verification.
834	Works under NO_PUBLIC_EGRESS (Private Google Access reaches Google APIs).
835	+
836	In GCB build step contexts (conjure, bind, graft, enshrine), registry authentication uses the GCE metadata server to obtain an access token and writes a static Docker config JSON.
837	+
838	Rationale: `gcloud` is an opaque, version-sensitive binary that obscures API semantics, introduces implicit authentication state, and breaks reproducibility.
839	The `gcloud auth configure-docker` approach also fails mechanically in GCB steps — the gcloud builder image lacks `docker` in PATH, so the credential helper it installs cannot function.
840	+
841	NOTE: The gcloud reliquary image exists because every ordain-path build step (conjure, bind, graft) uses it for the about/vouch pipeline (`bash`, `date`, and `gcloud` for provenance retrieval per the exception above).
842	The reliquary toolchain is mode-uniform: all ordain modes consume gcloud, docker, alpine, and syft from the reliquary; bind additionally uses skopeo for image-copy.
843	
844	[[rbsk_in_pool_preflight]]
845	//axl_voices axk_premise
846	{rbsk_in_pool_preflight}::
847	Every ordain-path Cloud Build job — conjure's combined image+about, bind's combined mirror+about, graft's combined about+vouch, and enshrine — prepends a step 0 (`rbgjr01-reliquary-preflight.sh`) that HEAD-checks reliquary tool images from the worker pool's vantage before expensive work runs.
848	Conjure additionally HEAD-checks the vessel's enshrined base anchors (slots 1-3 from `RBRV_IMAGE_n_ANCHOR`); other modes pass empty locator substitutions.
849	+
850	Coverage is 5 of 6 reliquary tools — gcloud, docker, syft, binfmt, skopeo.
851	Alpine, the preflight step's own image, is validated implicitly by Cloud Build pulling it before the script runs.
852	Failure produces a structured "this pool can't see «TOOL»" message naming each missed item, with remediation directive (re-inscribe + re-yoke + re-ordain).
853	+
854	Rationale: defense-in-depth on top of the host-side `zrbfd_registry_preflight` (which validates the Director's host-side view of GAR).
855	The in-pool check catches divergence the host-side check cannot — airgap private-VPC routing, Director↔Mason IAM split, and submit↔execute time skew.
856	+
857	Scope boundary: standalone vouch (`zrbfv_vouch_submit`) and standalone about (`zrbfv_about_submit`) are operator-recovery paths and are not currently covered.
858	
859	[[rbsk_invest_serialized]]
860	//axl_voices axk_premise
861	{rbsk_invest_serialized}::
862	{rbtgo_retriever_invest}, {rbtgo_director_invest}, and {rbtgo_governor_mantle} write credential bytes to a singleton assay slot (`RBDC_ASSAY_RBRA_FILE`), not a per-identity slot.
863	The operator MUST move `RBDC_ASSAY_RBRA_FILE` to a safe place before initiating the next operation that writes to the assay slot — typically the role's production slot (`RBDC_RETRIEVER_RBRA_FILE`, `RBDC_DIRECTOR_RBRA_FILE`, or `RBDC_GOVERNOR_RBRA_FILE`) when the invester and the consumer are the same operator, otherwise a delivery channel to the consumer.
864	Two consecutive assay-slot writers without an intervening move lose the first writer's bytes.
865	Batched invest is not supported — by design.
866	+

// ============================================================================
// TIER 1: WHY AND WHAT
// ============================================================================

== System Overview

=== Core Trust Challenge

Modern development increasingly requires running foreign code with access to local work.
{st_container}-based development tools processing source code, local AI/ML tools analyzing proprietary data, and third-party services needing controlled network access create fundamental security challenges.

This creates a fundamental trust dilemma:

*
{st_container_s}
provide powerful tool isolation
* But they need controlled access to do useful work
* Standard
{st_container}
networking grants unfettered outbound access or else network services completely unavailable.

=== The Bottle Pattern

{at_rbm_system}
solves this by creating two simple, robust boundaries around a certain
{st_container}
type here referred to as a
{at_bottle_container}:

. File Access Boundary
*
{at_bottle_container}
sees only explicitly controlled set of
{st_volume_mount_s}
* No other access to
{at_workstation}
filesystem

. Network Boundary
*
{at_bottle_container}
has no direct network access
* All traffic flows through dedicated
{at_sentry_container},
a
{st_container}
configured with very tight network access and visibility controls.
* Only declared
{st_port_map_s}
and destinations allowed
* Full logging of allowed and denied traffic

Each
{rbsc_crucible}
maintains these boundaries through a three-container architecture:

* One
{at_sentry_container}
enforcing network policy on dual networks (bridge and enclave)
* One
{at_pentacle_container}
establishing and configuring the network namespace
* One
{at_bottle_container}
running the actual service, sharing the pentacle's network namespace via compose `network_mode: "service:pentacle"`
* Clear configuration in a single
{at_nameplate}
file, extended by per-nameplate compose fragments for volume mounts

Docker Compose orchestrates the container lifecycle declaratively.
A base compose file (`rbob_compose.yml`) defines the three-container topology with health check chains: the
{at_sentry_container}
must be healthy before the
{at_pentacle_container}
starts, and the
{at_pentacle_container}
must be healthy before the
{at_bottle_container}
starts.
This prevents firewall bypass — the bottle cannot send traffic before security policies are enforced.
The "first packet" guarantee is enforceable because sentry's iptables are installed before the pentacle is healthy, the pentacle's namespace is shared with the bottle (so the bottle inherits the routing-and-firewall state already configured), and sentry sits on every inbound path by virtue of the published-port directive being on the sentry service (not pentacle) and the bottle's default route being via sentry.
Topology changes that violate any of these commitments require re-validation against the
{at_recipe_bottle}
Ifrit suite before declaring the security properties preserved.
Per-nameplate compose fragments (e.g., `«moniker».compose.yml`) add volume mount declarations.
Env-files passed via `--env-file` serve a dual purpose: YAML interpolation (`${VAR}` references in compose files resolve image refs, subnet, container names) and container environment forwarding (bare variable names in `environment:` sections inject values into containers at runtime).

The
{at_pentacle_container}
is a privileged container that configures routing to ensure all traffic flows through the
{at_sentry_container},
then shares this pre-configured namespace with the
{at_bottle_container}
via compose `network_mode: "service:pentacle"`.
This ensures security policies are enforced from the first packet, and the
{at_bottle_container}
experiences only a functional path to its
{at_sentry_container}
gateway

=== Security Properties

The
{at_rbm_system}
maintains following essential guarantees:

. File Access Control
*
{at_bottle_container_s}
can access only explicitly configured
{st_volume_mount_s}
* No access to broader system resources

. Network Isolation
* All traffic flows through dedicated
{at_sentry_container}
* Each permitted traffic flow maintains exactly one controlled pathway
* Precise ingress, forwarding, and NAT rules enforce strict boundaries
* Only explicitly configured
{st_port_map_s}
allow traffic between
{at_transit_network}
and
{at_enclave_network}
*
{at_bottle_container_s}
have no direct network access; all connectivity flows through
{at_sentry_container_s}
* Comprehensive traffic logging

. Service Separation
* Each
{rbsc_crucible}
fully isolated
* No cross-service communication
* Independent security policies
* Clean state separation

. Service Access Control
* External access to services only permitted when
{rbrn_entry_mode}
is enabled
* No inter-container communication allowed within pods
* All service access must originate from outside the pod
* Entry traffic flows: external -> pod ->
{at_sentry_container}
->
{at_bottle_container}

These properties hold across all operations and failure modes.
They depend on a layered-defense structure in which each layer is independently load-bearing for at least one invariant: network namespace isolation, capability bounds on the
{at_bottle_container}
(`cap_drop: NET_RAW, NET_ADMIN`), kernel reverse-path filtering, iptables filter chains (RBM-INGRESS, RBM-EGRESS, RBM-FORWARD), iptables NAT (PREROUTING DNAT, POSTROUTING MASQUERADE), and conntrack state tracking.
Removing or weakening a layer requires Ifrit-suite empirical validation to confirm the security envelope remains intact; architectural reasoning alone has been empirically insufficient.

=== Adversarial Validation: The Ifrit

include::RBSIP-ifrit_pentester.adoc[]

== Getting Started

For builders and operators new to
{at_recipe_bottle},
this section provides conceptual grounding and the setup journey.

include::RBSGS-GettingStarted.adoc[leveloffset=+2]

// ============================================================================
// TIER 2: HOW
// ============================================================================

== Google API Engagement

{at_recipe_bottle}
invokes
{gcp_service}
APIs through
{giam_service_account}
JWT authentication and direct HTTPS REST calls.

* *Authentication*:
{giam_service_account_s}
use private key credentials to sign JWTs with RS256.
These JWTs are exchanged for
{oauth_token_s}
via Google's token endpoint.
All API calls include the bearer token in the Authorization header.

* *Invocation Pattern*:
Operations execute through curl with JSON payloads and responses.
Each call captures both HTTP status codes and response bodies to files for processing.
Success is determined by HTTP 200/201/204 codes.

* *Long-Running Operations*:
{gcp_service}
{gcp_lro_s}
return operation IDs that must be polled until completion.
The system polls at configurable intervals until completion.
The system handles both success and error states.
Operations utilize
{rbbc_submit}
and
{rbbc_await}
to handle all asynchronous operations.

* *IAM Policies*:
All IAM policy modifications enforce version 3 to ensure conditional role binding support across all
{gcp_service}
resources.

* *IAM Member Format*:
All IAM member bindings use `serviceAccount:«EMAIL»` format.
+
GCP IAM policy `members` fields only accept email-format identifiers (`serviceAccount:name@project.iam.gserviceaccount.com`).
The numeric `uniqueId` returned by `serviceAccounts.create` is valid for *API URL paths* (e.g., `projects/-/serviceAccounts/«UNIQUE_ID»` for GET/verification) but *not* for policy binding members.
`serviceAccount:«NUMERIC_ID»` returns HTTP 400 "Invalid service account" on all setIamPolicy endpoints — project, repository, and bucket alike.
+
Google's documentation recommends numeric IDs "to identify a service account just after it is created" (link:https://docs.google.com/iam/docs/manage-access-service-accounts[Manage access to service accounts]), but this applies to API resource addressing, not to IAM policy member strings.
The internal IAM system resolves email→numeric transparently; callers must use email.
+
Google-provisioned service agents (Cloud Build) are not affected — they are managed by Google, not newly created.

== Operational Invariants

=== Concurrent Session Safety

{at_recipe_bottle}
commands are designed for concurrent execution from multiple independent sessions against the same filesystem.
This enables parallel workflows where different developers or automation systems invoke commands simultaneously.

Concurrency safety is achieved through:

* *Ephemeral Isolation*:
Each command invocation creates a unique temporary directory using `BURD_NOW_STAMP` (timestamp + process ID + random component), ensuring intermediate files never collide.

* *Unique Artifact Naming*:
Cloud resources (build IDs, inscribe timestamps) incorporate unique identifiers, preventing overwrite conflicts in
{rbtgi_repository}.

* *Read-Only Git Queries*:
Git operations are non-mutating (status checks, metadata reads).
The `git fetch` operation uses git's internal locking for safe concurrent execution.

* *Semantic Preconditions*:
Operations like
{rbtgo_ark_conjure}
require clean git state.
This is a workflow constraint ensuring reproducible builds, not a collision guard.

NOTE: Concurrent builds of the same vessel produce independent images with different timestamps.
Both builds succeed; neither overwrites the other.

NOTE: The
{gcb_service}
private pool enforces a *Concurrent Build CPUs (Private Pool)* quota per project per region.
Machine type is set at pool creation time via
{rbrd_gcb_machine_type}
(a Compute Engine type such as `e2-standard-2`); individual build requests do not specify machine type.
If the pool's vCPU allocation is nearly exhausted, a second
build invocation will remain in `QUEUED` status while the first is `WORKING`.
The
{rbrr_gcb_min_concurrent_builds}
regime variable declares the operator's minimum concurrency requirement.
See
{rbtgo_quota_build}
for the manual Console procedure to review build capacity settings.

== Key Premises

[[rbsk_no_gcloud]]
//axl_voices axk_premise
{rbsk_no_gcloud}::
{at_recipe_bottle}
scripts MUST NOT invoke the `gcloud` CLI in local bash scripts or transitively through helper scripts.
All local
{gcp_service}
interaction uses direct REST API calls via `curl` with JWT-authenticated bearer tokens.
+
**Exception — vouch provenance fetch**: The {rbtgo_ark_vouch} verification step (conjure mode only) invokes `gcloud artifacts docker images describe --show-provenance` for SLSA provenance envelope retrieval.
This wraps complex Container Analysis API interaction that would be burdensome to replicate via raw REST.
All other GCB step operations use the GCE metadata server for OAuth token acquisition and direct registry REST API calls — no gcloud.
The provenance fetch runs as Python 3 on the gcloud builder image after the primary image is built; the reproducibility concern that motivates this premise is weaker for post-build verification.
Works under NO_PUBLIC_EGRESS (Private Google Access reaches Google APIs).
+
In GCB build step contexts (conjure, bind, graft, enshrine), registry authentication uses the GCE metadata server to obtain an access token and writes a static Docker config JSON.
+
Rationale: `gcloud` is an opaque, version-sensitive binary that obscures API semantics, introduces implicit authentication state, and breaks reproducibility.
The `gcloud auth configure-docker` approach also fails mechanically in GCB steps — the gcloud builder image lacks `docker` in PATH, so the credential helper it installs cannot function.
+
NOTE: The gcloud reliquary image exists because every ordain-path build step (conjure, bind, graft) uses it for the about/vouch pipeline (`bash`, `date`, and `gcloud` for provenance retrieval per the exception above).
The reliquary toolchain is mode-uniform: all ordain modes consume gcloud, docker, alpine, and syft from the reliquary; bind additionally uses skopeo for image-copy.

[[rbsk_in_pool_preflight]]
//axl_voices axk_premise
{rbsk_in_pool_preflight}::
Every ordain-path Cloud Build job — conjure's combined image+about, bind's combined mirror+about, graft's combined about+vouch, and enshrine — prepends a step 0 (`rbgjr01-reliquary-preflight.sh`) that HEAD-checks reliquary tool images from the worker pool's vantage before expensive work runs.
Conjure additionally HEAD-checks the vessel's enshrined base anchors (slots 1-3 from `RBRV_IMAGE_n_ANCHOR`); other modes pass empty locator substitutions.
+
Coverage is 5 of 6 reliquary tools — gcloud, docker, syft, binfmt, skopeo.
Alpine, the preflight step's own image, is validated implicitly by Cloud Build pulling it before the script runs.
Failure produces a structured "this pool can't see «TOOL»" message naming each missed item, with remediation directive (re-inscribe + re-yoke + re-ordain).
+
Rationale: defense-in-depth on top of the host-side `zrbfd_registry_preflight` (which validates the Director's host-side view of GAR).
The in-pool check catches divergence the host-side check cannot — airgap private-VPC routing, Director↔Mason IAM split, and submit↔execute time skew.
+
Scope boundary: standalone vouch (`zrbfv_vouch_submit`) and standalone about (`zrbfv_about_submit`) are operator-recovery paths and are not currently covered.

[[rbsk_invest_serialized]]
//axl_voices axk_premise
{rbsk_invest_serialized}::
{rbtgo_retriever_invest}, {rbtgo_director_invest}, and {rbtgo_governor_mantle} write credential bytes to a singleton assay slot (`RBDC_ASSAY_RBRA_FILE`), not a per-identity slot.
The operator MUST move `RBDC_ASSAY_RBRA_FILE` to a safe place before initiating the next operation that writes to the assay slot — typically the role's production slot (`RBDC_RETRIEVER_RBRA_FILE`, `RBDC_DIRECTOR_RBRA_FILE`, or `RBDC_GOVERNOR_RBRA_FILE`) when the invester and the consumer are the same operator, otherwise a delivery channel to the consumer.
Two consecutive assay-slot writers without an intervening move lose the first writer's bytes.
Batched invest is not supported — by design.
+

Got the lexicon — this project speaks a very specific dialect: alchemical/heraldic (Crucible, Pentacle, Sigil, Hallmark, Reliquary, Ifrit, Ordain, Conjure, Vouch, Enshrine, Abjure) layered over a heraldic org chart (Governor, Payor, Director, Retriever, Marshal, Mason). And the actual problem domain is *bottling foreign code behind file + network boundaries.*

One thing worth flagging up front: **Docker Hub** already exists and is *literally* a container registry. So a plain "ContainerHub" lands right on top of the most obvious incumbent. The playful move is to lean into your own weird lexicon so the `*hub` is unmistakably yours, not a Docker Hub knockoff.

Here's a spread, grouped by which thread they pull:

## Lean into the bottle / containment metaphor
- **BottleHub** — most on-brand; the bottle *is* the core pattern. Reads clean.
- **CorkHub** — what seals the bottle. Cute, short.
- **CellarHub** — where bottles are stored/aged. Nice double meaning (jail cell + wine cellar).
- **DecanterHub** — pouring controlled access out of the bottle.
- **FlaskHub** — alchemist's vessel; bonus nerd-nod to the Python framework.

## Lean into the alchemy / ritual layer
- **CrucibleHub** — your central runtime concept; sounds serious and a little dangerous.
- **CauldronHub** — the playful cousin of Crucible; very "we brew dangerous things safely."
- **ConjureHub** — verb-forward, matches your `conjure` build mode.
- **SigilHub** — heraldic, mysterious, short.
- **WardHub** — a ward is a protective enchantment *and* a guarded enclosure (and you already have Ward Realm Substrate). Strong containment double meaning.

## Lean into the security / sentry layer
- **SentryHub** — but Sentry.io exists (error monitoring), so collision risk.
- **EnclaveHub** — your enclave network; sounds like a secure-compute product.
- **GarrisonHub** — heraldic, "a fortified holding."
- **MoatHub** — playful, instantly communicates "boundary."

## The deliberately cheeky / "pornhub-energy" picks
These are the ones that make people grin (and remember):
- **ContainerHub** — the obvious one you started with (just beware Docker Hub).
- **BottleHub** — doubles as the safe-and-marketable cheeky pick.
- **PentacleHub** — sounds like a summoning ritual went enterprise. Very funny given the actual containment theme.
- **IfritHub** — name it after your *attacker*. Audacious. "We built a hub around the demon we cage."

## My shortlist if you actually pursued it
1. **BottleHub** — truest to the core pattern, marketable, mildly cheeky.
2. **CrucibleHub** — gravitas, ties to your runtime, sounds like a security product.
3. **WardHub** — best containment double-meaning, already echoes WRS.
4. **PentacleHub** — if you want the wink-and-grin branding energy specifically.

Want me to push any of these further — e.g., riff on taglines, check rough domain/namespace collisions, or generate a second wave seeded from one direction you like?

content.text:
Oh, you've stumbled onto the *perfect* myth for this project, and it's not even a stretch — it's the literal origin story.

## The Brazen Vessel of Solomon
In the *Ars Goetia*, Solomon bound 72 demons inside a **brass vessel** sealed with a **sigil**, and cast it into a lake. Babylonians found it, assumed treasure, cracked it open, and released the demons.

That is your security parable *exactly*: foreign code is the demon, the **bottle** is the brazen vessel, the **sigil** seals it (you already have `RBRV_SIGIL`!), and "don't crack the vessel open" is the whole threat model. You don't need to invent the demon theme — your lexicon (Ifrit, Pentacle, Sigil, Conjure, Abjure, Vessel) *already encodes it*. You've been building a grimoire and didn't fully name it.

## Demon-binding `*hub` ideas

**The summoner / binder picks** (you summon it, then control it):
- **FamiliarHub** — a *familiar* is a bound spirit-servant. Untrusted code as your obedient familiar. Genuinely lovely metaphor.
- **BrazenHub** — the brass vessel itself; "brazen" also means bold/shameless. Cheeky *and* mythologically exact.
- **GoetiaHub** — the grimoire of binding. Esoteric, sounds like a serious security product.
- **SolomonHub** — Solomon *is* the archetypal demon-binder, and "Solomon" connotes wisdom (good for a trust product). Bonus in-joke: Docker's founder is Solomon Hykes.

**The containment-circle picks** (pentacle's cousins):
- **PentacleHub** — your word; sounds like a summoning went enterprise.
- **CircleHub** / **WardHub** — the protective circle / ward.
- **TriangleHub** — the *Triangle of Art*, where the demon is forced to manifest, constrained. (Niche but accurate.)

**The daemon puns** (you'll either love or hate):
- **DaemonHub** — UNIX `daemon` *is* "demon." On-the-nose, probably collision-prone.
- **HexHub** — hex = curse + hexadecimal. Short, alliterative, code-flavored.
- **ImpHub** — minor demon + "imp" as in *implement*. Tiny and cute.

**The jinn thread (most native to your code — Ifrit is a jinn!):**
- **DjinnHub** / **JinnHub** — genie-in-a-*bottle*. Unites your bottle core *and* your Ifrit attacker under one mythology. This might be the most internally-coherent pick of all.

## On the expensive domains
That's the tell that the *common* English words are gone. The cheap, available ones are the **coined / esoteric grimoire terms** — `goetia`, `brazen`, `djinn`, `familiar`, made-up compounds. The more obscure the demonology, the likelier `.com`/`.dev`/`.io` is sitting there for $12.

My ranked four if you're serious: **BrazenHub** (myth-exact + cheeky), **DjinnHub** (unifies bottle + Ifrit), **FamiliarHub** (best metaphor), **GoetiaHub** (most product-serious).

Want me to actually check domain availability/pricing on a handful of these?
